core_story.summary: In the mod- ern digital era, simulating realistic facial skin reflectance is still the key challenge in computer vision and computer This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation. However, this approach faces two significant limi- tations. This motivates us to explore whether the extensive priors captured in recent gen- erative diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) can enable more generalizable facial reflectance estimation as these models have been pre-trained on large-scale internet im- age collections containing rich visual patterns. In this pa- per, we introduce the use of Stable Diffusion as a prior for facial reflectance estimation, achieving robust results with minimal captured data for fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves strong generalization and produces high-fidelity, SSS-compliant facial reflectance estimation.

problem_expression: High-quality 3D facial reflectance is critical to achieving lifelike 3D face rendering under varied lighting conditions, a goal that continues to push the boundaries of technology.
limitation_expression: However, limited subject diver- sity in these datasets poses challenges in achieving good generalization and broad applicability.

In the mod- ern digital era, simulating realistic facial skin reflectance is still the key challenge in computer vision and computer This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
However, this approach faces two significant limi- tations.
Second, most current methods learn from in-the-wild data, resulting in biased lighting estimation.

However, capturing such high-fidelity facial reflectance data is both costly and labor-intensive, necessitating specialized scan- ning equipment (e.g., Light Stage [6, 15]) and skilled artists for post-processing.

Therefore, recent datasets [26, 29, 43] have been limited to fewer than three hundred subjects, constraining the generalizability of these reflectance mod- els [26, 29, 33, 43] across diverse real-world identities.
Recent work, ID2Reflectance [38], em- ploys color image priors and limited captured data to sup- port reflectance model learning, yet it struggles with mid- frequency detail retention.
Accordingly, we leverage the Stable Diffusion model as a prior, fine-tuning it on a limited set of reflectance data.
insight_expression: This motivates us to explore whether the extensive priors captured in recent gen- erative diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) can enable more generalizable facial reflectance estimation as these models have been pre-trained on large-scale internet im- age collections containing rich visual patterns.
method_expression: Our method adopts a two-stage training approach: in the first stage, DSN-Net is trained to predict diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and normal maps from in-the-wild images using a novel joint reflectance attention module.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves strong generalization and produces high-fidelity, SSS-compliant facial reflectance estimation.
Our method produces de- tailed diffuse albedo, specular albedo, normal, hemoglobin, and melanin maps.

Our method not only supports SSS-complaint rendering but also has potential applications in skin disease diagnosis. • We introduce a two-stage reflectance estimation ap- proach: the first stage predicts diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and normal maps from in-the-wild images using a novel joint reflectance attention module, while the sec- ond stage further predicts hemoglobin and melanin maps from the diffuse albedo, resulting in SSS-compliant and detailed reflectance maps. • Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed method generates high-fidelity facial reflectance maps, producing realistic rendering effects.

Experimen- tal results demonstrate that our method excels in both qual- itative and quantitative evaluations, showcasing strong gen- eralization to real-world images and delivering accurate, de- tailed reflectance reconstructions.
Nevertheless, our method enables high-quality face rendering, facilitates rapid avatar creation for metaverse applications, and offers advanced solutions for dermatological research.
experiment_expression: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves strong generalization and produces high-fidelity, SSS-compliant facial reflectance estimation.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Recent 3D face reconstruction methods have made remark-
able advancements, yet achieving high-quality facial re-
flectance from monocular input remains challenging. Exist-
ing methods rely on the light-stage captured data to learn
facial reflectance models. However, limited subject diver-
sity in these datasets poses challenges in achieving good
generalization and broad applicability. This motivates us to
explore whether the extensive priors captured in recent gen-
erative diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) can enable
more generalizable facial reflectance estimation as these
models have been pre-trained on large-scale internet im-
age collections containing rich visual patterns. In this pa-
per, we introduce the use of Stable Diffusion as a prior
for facial reflectance estimation, achieving robust results
with minimal captured data for fine-tuning. We present
S3-Face, a comprehensive framework capable of produc-
*Corresponding authors
ing SSS-compliant skin reflectance from in-the-wild images.
Our method adopts a two-stage training approach: in the
first stage, DSN-Net is trained to predict diffuse albedo,
specular albedo, and normal maps from in-the-wild images
using a novel joint reflectance attention module. In the sec-
ond stage, HM-Net is trained to generate hemoglobin and
melanin maps based on the diffuse albedo predicted in the
first stage, yielding SSS-compliant and detailed reflectance
maps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method
achieves strong generalization and produces high-fidelity,
SSS-compliant facial reflectance estimation.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Long before the digital age, artists meticulously strove to
capture the appearance of real skin in oil paintings, as il-
lustrated in Fig. 1, relying on sharp observational skills and
an intimate understanding of light and texture. In the mod-
ern digital era, simulating realistic facial skin reflectance
is still the key challenge in computer vision and computer
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
16051

graphics. High-quality 3D facial reflectance is critical to
achieving lifelike 3D face rendering under varied lighting
conditions, a goal that continues to push the boundaries of
technology. This endeavor supports a wide range of ap-
plications, including avatar generation [53], telecommuni-
cation [34], video games, film production, and immersive
AR/VR experiences.
Accurate facial reflectance estimation from a single im-
age remains essential, with the principal challenge being to
capture the complex details of skin reflectance and to nav-
igate the intricacies of realistic rendering approximations.
Existing methods for facial reflectance reconstruction pri-
marily fall into two categories. The first approach generally
assumes a bidirectional reflectance distribution function
(BRDF), optimizing the material and lighting for in-the-
wild images through differentiable rendering techniques.
Common BRDF approximations [7, 12] span from the sim-
ple Lambert model [45] (capturing only diffuse reflectance)
to more advanced models ( e.g., Cook-Torrance [5] and
Disney [3] models). Recently, MoSAR [10] has intro-
duced microfacet BRDF [50] for improved rendering re-
sults. However, this approach faces two significant limi-
tations. First, current BRDF models inadequately capture
the unique properties of skin, which involves both reflec-
tion and transmission. Accurate skin rendering requires
subsurface scattering (SSS), typically represented through
a bidirectional scattering distribution function (BSDF), yet
no differentiable renderer used for facial reflectance re-
construction currently supports these complex calculations.
Second, most current methods learn from in-the-wild data,
resulting in biased lighting estimation. Although more
physically grounded, these methods struggle to capture re-
alistic reflectance details due to these limitations, ultimately
resulting in less accurate rendering outcomes.
The second class of methods treats face reflectance esti-
mation as an intrinsic properties decomposition [25] prob-
lem and adopts a data-driven approach to solve it. To
achieve a realistic facial reflectance model, it is necessary
to collect a large-scale and high-quality reflectance dataset
encompassing a diverse range of individuals. However,
capturing such high-fidelity facial reflectance data is both
costly and labor-intensive, necessitating specialized scan-
ning equipment (e.g., Light Stage [6, 15]) and skilled artists
for post-processing. Therefore, recent datasets [26, 29, 43]
have been limited to fewer than three hundred subjects,
constraining the generalizability of these reflectance mod-
els [26, 29, 33, 43] across diverse real-world identities.
To this end, AvatarMe++ [27] employs an image-to-image
translation network [20] to synthesize diffuse, specular, and
normal maps from large-scale facial texture maps [2]. Uti-
lizing complete facial reflectance pairs, FitMe [28] and Re-
lightify [35] leverage StyleGAN [22] and latent diffusion
models [39] to effectively learn facial reflectance priors,
enhancing reconstruction performance. Nonetheless, the
facial reflectance data from these studies [27, 28, 35] re-
main unreleased. Recent work, ID2Reflectance [38], em-
ploys color image priors and limited captured data to sup-
port reflectance model learning, yet it struggles with mid-
frequency detail retention.
In this paper, we present S 3-Face, a comprehensive fa-
cial reflectance estimation method that generates skin re-
flectance consistent with subsurface scattering (SSS) prop-
erties from in-the-wild images. Our method produces de-
tailed diffuse albedo, specular albedo, normal, hemoglobin,
and melanin maps. Real-world face images are the results
of a complex interplay of lighting and facial skin, and de-
composing the face appearance into lighting and reflectance
properties is a highly ambiguous task, as lighting can be
baked into albedos and vice versa. We posit that modern
image diffusion models, trained on vast, Internet-scale im-
age collections, encapsulate generalizable visual patterns
that can support the development of a broadly applicable
reflectance estimator. Accordingly, we leverage the Stable
Diffusion model as a prior, fine-tuning it on a limited set of
reflectance data.
Specifically, we fine-tune the Stable Diffusion model
in two stages. In the first stage, we adapt the original
UNet architecture by introducing a joint reflectance atten-
tion module, enabling geometry-consistent estimation of
diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and normal maps from in-
the-wild images. In the second stage, we employ the pre-
dicted diffuse albedo to further estimate the hemoglobin and
melanin maps. These architectural enhancements, coupled
with our two-stage fine-tuning approach, enable S3-Face to
achieve high-quality reflectance estimation, including the
pioneering extraction of hemoglobin and melanin informa-
tion from raw input images. Finally, we develop a compre-
hensive pipeline that reconstructs high-quality reflectance
maps from various input types, facilitating high-fidelity ren-
dering across diverse lighting conditions. In summary, we
make the following contributions:
• For the first time, we achieve the hemoglobin and melanin
maps prediction from in-the-wild images. Our method
not only supports SSS-complaint rendering but also has
potential applications in skin disease diagnosis.
• We introduce a two-stage reflectance estimation ap-
proach: the first stage predicts diffuse albedo, specular
albedo, and normal maps from in-the-wild images using
a novel joint reflectance attention module, while the sec-
ond stage further predicts hemoglobin and melanin maps
from the diffuse albedo, resulting in SSS-compliant and
detailed reflectance maps.
• Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed
method generates high-fidelity facial reflectance maps,
producing realistic rendering effects.
16052
raw_sections.method:
3. Methodology
This work aims to generate high-quality skin reflectance
maps ( i.e., diffuse albedo, specular albedo, normal,
melanin, and hemoglobin) from a single facial input im-
age x ∈ Rh×w×3 for photorealistic rendering. We first
revisit the SSS-compliant skin reflectance model and the la-
tent diffusion model (Sec. 3.1). Since facial hemoglobin
and melanin are highly sensitive to lighting conditions, ob-
taining accurate results from a single model is challenging.
To this end, we introduce a two-stage approach (Sec. 3.2),
which improves the stability and convergence of the model.
In the first stage, we train a DSN-Net (Fig. 3) to predict dif-
fuse albedo, specular albedo, and normal images from the
color images. In the second stage, we train another HM-Net
(Fig. 5) to derive hemoglobin and melanin assets from the
estimated diffuse albedo. Finally, we present the pipeline to
derive complete facial UV reflectance maps using our two-
stage model (Sec. 3.3).
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusions and Discussions
In this paper, we introduced a novel method for SSS-
compliant facial reflectance estimation that leverages a pre-
trained Stable Diffusion model as a powerful prior. Com-
bined with our two-stage training paradigm and joint re-
flectance attention module, our approach achieves robust,
high-quality facial reflectance generation using minimal
captured data. The SSS-compliant facial reflectance esti-
mation supports not only photorealistic rendering but also
opens new avenues for skin disease diagnosis. Experimen-
tal results demonstrate that our method excels in both qual-
itative and quantitative evaluations, showcasing strong gen-
eralization to real-world images and delivering accurate, de-
tailed reflectance reconstructions. Broader Impacts. Al-
though not the purpose of our work, facial reflectance may
potentially be abused. Nevertheless, our method enables
high-quality face rendering, facilitates rapid avatar creation
for metaverse applications, and offers advanced solutions
for dermatological research.
